data.title: QXO: Invest In The Man And Mission
data.description:
Investment Thesis

With the leadership of Brad Jacobs and an industry ripe for change, QXO is a top long-term growth pick as they look to get $50 billion in annual revenue within the next decade. Right now, QXO is in a prime position for growth, and the market is factoring in some, but not nearly enough.

The x-factor in this company is its leader, Brad Jacobs, who has one of the best track records on Wall Street. The most notable include United Rentals, a 200-bagger, and XPO, a 50-bagger. Famous for his playbook, he builds massive amounts of shareholder value by acquiring and consolidating fragmented, high-growth industries.

This time, he hit the nail on the head. The targeted industry is extremely fragmented, leverages macro tailwinds, and is lagging on technology. This gives Mr. Jacobs the opportunity to execute his playbook to perfection and, once again, create outsized returns.

They acquired Beacon Roofing in April of this year, targeting a company with demand tailwinds and less cyclicality. Progress has been made through modernization, providing productivity gains and margin improvement by leveraging AI.

Although the market is pricing in some growth, given QXO trades at higher multiples than peers, it isn't pricing in the full potential of a Jacobs-led venture. Currently, QXO is just beginning its journey, making an attractive entry point while its growth potential is undervalued.

Overview

QXO was originally founded in late 2023 with the goal of consolidating the fragmented building materials' distribution industry using scale, technology, and acquisitions.

Mr. Jacobs, the founder and CEO, is known for his rigorous, intense research on M&A targets and will never overpay. He often leverages the newest and best advancements in technology, forming a moat against competitors. People are also a key part of his strategy, as he only wants "A-players," ones who he couldn't imagine working without and is more than happy to overpay to keep strong talent on board. Using his repeatable, successful playbook, QXO has all it needs to be a major player.

By July 2024, they were able to form a war chest of $5 billion, which included an investment from Jared Kushner's Affinity Partners. Kushner, the President's son-in-law, was also placed on the QXO board of directors.

Although their initial target, Rexel, rejected their bid, they were able to acquire Beacon Roofing, the largest publicly traded roofing company in North America. This was a great first addition as Beacon offers limited tariff exposure, demand from severe weather events, and opportunities from repair and remodel activity. This gives Beacon less cyclicality through housing cycles since roofs aren't optional, making the demand sticky.

Since the acquisition, they have made one other offer for GMS, a major drywall, ceiling, and steel framing distributor, for $5 billion. However, they were outbid shortly after by Home Depot, leaving a warning sign for QXO as competitors look to slow their acquisition progress.

QXO has now had just over six months to work with their first acquisition, and in the Q2 report, Mr. Jacobs mentions, "The integration of Beacon is progressing well, and we've identified opportunities that exceed our initial expectations." After getting under the hood of Beacon, QXO realized there were more opportunities to improve than they realized. This included many of their top SKUs being out of stock, a lack of warehouse automation, and $200 million in pricing leakage. They have begun addressing these problems directly, leveraging AI and data to modernize systems and improve pricing.

Industry

To put it bluntly, the $800 billion building products' distribution industry was chosen for a reason.

First, the industry is highly fragmented. In North America alone, there are ≈7,000 distributors, leaving it ripe for consolidation. As the company scales and pursues more acquisitions, expect synergies to improve margins and growth to compound.

The industry is also lagging behind in technology, and when acquiring Beacon, they found that fast-moving SKUs (making up 80% of sales) were often out of stock, outdated systems, manual processes, and limited inventory visibility. An estimated $200 million was lost to inefficiencies at Beacon; however, this is now being addressed as Mr. Jacobs claims double-digit productivity gains, largely through AI, and improved pricing discipline. This lines up with what Mr. Jacobs did at his previous venture, XPO, where he implemented changes such as autonomous tech, real-time visibility, dynamic pricing, and route optimization.

The tailwinds also make the industry attractive, with multiple demand drivers expected to help sales. One of these includes the current housing shortage, estimated to be ≈4 million homes in the U.S. With the current administration's push for affordable housing, QXO should be a large benefactor. The current housing pool also has a median age around 40 years old, providing additional demand opportunity through remodeling and repair. Finally, with an expected increase in climate-related disasters, building materials will also be needed for repair. This isn't just another Jacobs venture; it's change coming to an industry with secular tailwinds and a need for advancement.

Strategic Hires

A large part of Mr. Jacobs's philosophy is having the right team around him and utilizing the latest technology to improve productivity. Recently, these have gone hand-in-hand, with the hiring of a Chief AI Officer, Ashwin Rao. These types of hires are on the rise, mostly in tech and healthcare, but lag in the building materials distribution industry. Prior to his current position, Rao served as the first head of AI at Target Corp. "where he led a team that developed mathematical models for pricing, merchandising, customer experience, supply chain logistics, and other critical functions." This directly translates into QXO's technological mission of wholly upgrading the current technology and using data to their advantage.

More recently, they hired Michael Dewitt as the chief procurement officer, who came after working eight years as the VP of International Spend Management at Walmart International. There, he drove billions in savings and was one of the first to use AI for sourcing. His initiatives of next-generation procurement technologies "more than doubled annual savings in three years." This will correlate directly with an industry that has lagged on many of these technologies for years.

Recent Results

So far, they have progressed through two quarters with Beacon, beating EPS and revenue in both quarters.

Mr. Jacobs likes to move fast, and this case is no exception. They made huge strides in Q2, such as rebranding every customer touchpoint, flattening organizational structure from nine to four layers, stricter pricing tools to protect against leakage, linking compensation to profitability, and implementing immediate replenishments of critical SKUs. Remember, those are just a few of the changes they've made so far.

In Q2, the press release mentioned they are "confident we will at least double legacy Beacon EBITDA organically." They maintained that outlook in Q3, stating they "are firmly on track to organically grow legacy Beacon's EBITDA to more than $2 billion." Beacon had more issues than they initially thought; however, this isn't a setback. It's a realization of increased opportunity leading to a higher goal. To ultimately reach $2 billion in EBITDA, QXO is leveraging technology such as warehouse automation, something used at XPO, as well as utilizing AI. Double-digit productivity gains have already been provided by utilizing AI-focused tech in inventory management, pricing, and logistics. All of this provides support for not only their Beacon EBITDA target, but also their overall goal of $50 billion in annual revenue.

This has gone under the radar, with the stock remaining flat since June, ignoring results from both quarters under QXO management. With the stated target and more-than-expected opportunity, the current valuation is only more attractive.

Analysts tend to be very bullish on the stock with an average price target of $32.20 (+51%) and no ratings below buy (19% buy, 81% strong buy). This comes with higher expectations, as analysts expect QXO to grow EBITDA at a 34% CAGR over the next five years versus the S&P 500 Industrials Index, which has grown at a 14% CAGR over the previous five years. Both Wolfe and Oppenheimer expect $4.5 billion in EBITDA by 2030, led by best-in-class technology for the industry and familiarity with the Jacobs' playbook.

Valuation

Originally, Beacon was bought for $11 billion, and QXO is currently worth ≈$15 billion, adding a premium for the hope of improved growth and future acquisitions. While QXO is still integrating Beacon, hindering margins, it trades at a forward P/E of 45.25x and a forward P/S of 1.4x. These metrics don't tell the full story, making the best measure of valuation EV/EBITDA.

For 2026, revenue is expected to be $10.86 billion, and in the most recent quarter, adjusted EBITDA margin came in at 11%. Assuming margin improvement, driven by technology investments and productivity gains, we can spread that 11% for FY26, getting EBITDA of $1.3 billion. Currently, S&P 500 Industrials trade at an average of 17x EBITDA, while growth from 2020 to 2025 was at a 14% CAGR. QXO is projected to grow EBITDA at a 34% CAGR through 2030, leaving room to demand a higher multiple of 20x. With outstanding shares of 674.4 million and net debt of $1.64 billion, the equity value comes to $24.4 billion, or a price per share of ≈$36.20. As a base case, that's my 12-month price target.

Risks

QXO has had some helpful surprises so far, including the improved opportunity at Beacon. However, they have also had some setbacks. This has been driven by Lowe's and Home Depot, which have attempted to slow acquisition progress. Home Depot outbid them for GMS, their third attempt at an acquisition. Just a few months after, Lowe's acquired their own building materials' distributor, Foundation Building Materials, for $8.8 billion in another attempt to slow QXO. Although this is something to consider, the industry is still highly fragmented and allows room for QXO to continue acquisitions. Home Depot and Lowe's could keep up the pursuit; however, it would come at the expense of their margins, something they might not be able to give up.

To achieve their goal of doubling organic EBITDA for Beacon, they will have to follow through on execution, something Jacobs and his team are accustomed to. During their logistics venture, XPO, they doubled two of their acquisitions' profits in three years. Although their goal for QXO is entirely possible, unexpected pressures from housing or weather could hinder their progress.

If unexpected macro pressures occur, acquisition progress could slow down their overall goal of $50 billion in sales by 2035. However, the experience of this team should not be underestimated, with 40 years of experience working through all types of economic conditions, they're unlikely to be stopped.

Conclusion

Brad Jacobs has used his playbook to create outsized returns and billions of dollars for over 40 years, with QXO being his latest venture. He has shown focus and commitment to QXO's goals by stepping down from chairman roles at GXO and XPO. Not only is the industry highly fragmented, but strong tailwinds exist due to a lack of housing, aged housing stock, and expected climate change. I highly recommend listening to a discussion with Mr. Jacobs about QXO and the industry on this Bloomberg podcast.

Strong progress has already been made with the acquisition and integration of Beacon Roofing with changes such as modernizing systems, warehouse management systems, and leakage protection. Additional opportunities have been identified, and Beacon EBITDA is expected to double organically over the coming years, solidifying their goal of $50 billion in sales annually in the next decade.

Unexpected competition has come from Home Depot and Lowe's, which have bought companies in QXO's acquisition sphere these past few months. It is something to consider; however, this can't be kept up forever with the scale of fragmentation in the building materials' industry. With a current war chest of $2.31 billion in cash and leverage potential, they are ready for acquisitions.

As a base case, factoring in Beacon improvement and some M&A activity, my price target for the next twelve months is ≈$36.20 per share, or an equity value of $24.4 billion. This isn't something new to Jacobs and company, as they've pulled off similar feats in the forms of XPO, United Rentals, and United Waste Systems. That's why I'm not just investing in the mission; I'm investing in the man.

data.title: Will Santa Claus Rally For Top AI Stocks?
data.description:
Registration is now open for our Top Stocks for 2026 event with Steven Cress, featuring his newest list of high-conviction picks designed to target long-term performance. Steve’s previous Top Stocks lists have demonstrated an impressive track record, so don’t miss out on this much-anticipated event.

Santa Rally 2025: AI Edition

The “Santa Claus rally” is the seasonal tendency for stocks to rise over the final five trading days of the year and the first two of the new year. Since 1950, this seven-day window has delivered positive returns roughly 77% of the time, with an average S&P 500 gain of about 1.3%, making it one of the market’s most consistently strong periods. Moreover, since 1950, the Santa Claus rally has never failed to occur three years in a row. After back‑to‑back losing years for the index during this period, the natural question is whether Santa’s magic will return this time around.

Source Link: Seeking Alpha

Since I wrote 3 Best AI Stocks For The 2025 Santa Rally, markets have been anything but quiet. The Fed delivered its third rate cut of 2025 and signaled that there would likely be only one additional rate cut in 2026. After a sharp pullback on Dec. 12, it appeared a rotation into defensive sectors might come to pass. However, equities quickly found their footing on recent CPI data that reinforced the disinflation narrative. Against this backdrop, the question for 2025 isn’t just whether the rally shows up again, but which segments of the market will continue to flourish in 2026?

One High‑Potential Cannabis Pick for Your Stocking

The Santa Claus Rally typically favors the year’s existing leadership, and AI and data center names are well positioned to benefit from the seasonal pattern. However, this year also brings a new twist as cannabis stocks have a major policy catalyst in President Donald Trump’s executive order pushing to reschedule cannabis from Schedule I to Schedule III. Before diving into potential AI names, SA Quant has provided a bonus cannabis stock given the recent news.

Village Farms International, Inc. (VFF)

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Agricultural Products & Services

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 1 out of 177

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 1 out of 11

  • Market Capitalization: $417.02M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

VFF has recently pulled back in a classic sell-the-news pattern following Trump’s rescheduling announcement, but that volatility does not diminish the stock’s underlying fundamental case. VFF runs large-scale greenhouses that grow and distribute fresh produce in North America and also operates cannabis businesses in Canada, the U.S., and select international markets. The company delivered a record Q3 2025, driven by strong Canadian cannabis performance and surging international medical exports. Management outlined an expansion of Canadian production capacity and positioned Europe as a key growth driver over the next few years. This is consistent with what we’re seeing from a Quant perspective, where FWD EBITDA growth stands at an impressive near 95% compared to the sector’s 4.45%.

VFF Growth Grade

These promising developments have helped support positive analyst revisions, reinforcing the bullish narrative around the stock. That positive sentiment has contributed to powerful price momentum, with shares delivering a remarkable 367% return over the past year.

VFF Momentum Grade

While the stock’s headline valuation screens at a modest premium to the sector, it still trades at a meaningful double‑digit discount on a forward GAAP P/E basis, offering a more attractive entry point than first glance might suggest. VFF’s blend of growth potential and financial resilience highlights its ability to turn one kind of greenery into another, with enticing implications for cannabis investors.

How I Chose My Top AI Stocks

For readers who have been with us for some time, it’s no surprise that many AI-related opportunities span multiple sectors and industries, so it can be limiting to focus on a single sector. With that in mind, I screened IT stocks using the Seeking Alpha screener by selecting “Sector & Industry,” layering on all five Factor Grades (Valuation, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and EPS Revisions), then sorting for Strong Buys with B+ or better Momentum. I also revisited earlier picks from this year’s AI lists and checked them twice for continued Quant conviction.

1. Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO)

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductors

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 8 out of 537

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 2 out of 68

  • Market Capitalization: $27.08B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

Credo has earned the distinction as my top-performing 2025 Alpha Pick and also ranks as one of my best-performing Top Stocks for 2025. The company has remained out of reach for many investors, having maintained a Quant Hold rating for the majority of July through November owing to its rich valuation. However, recent market volatility and its latest earnings have once again brought the stock back within reach. CRDO had a monster Q2 FY26, delivering $268 million in revenue, up 20% sequentially and 272% year-over-year with a 68% non-GAAP gross margin. The company’s growth story continues to impress, according to CRDO President, CEO and Chairman William Brennan:

“In summary, we now have five distinct high-growth connectivity pillars: AECs, IC solutions, including retimers and optical DSPs, zero-flap optics, ALCs and OmniConnect Gearbox solutions. Together, they'll give Credo a combined total market opportunity that we believe will exceed $10 billion in the coming years, more than triple where we stood just 18 months ago.”

​This is consistent from a Quant perspective, with the company showcasing an overall ‘A+’ Growth Grade supported by an EPS FWD Long Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) that's nearly 335% above the sector median. CRDO has also demonstrated steady improvement in its profitability characteristics, climbing from a ‘D’ grade just six months ago to its current 'B.' The company possesses a net income margin that's nearly 449% above the sector median, underscoring Credo’s operating leverage and pricing power.

CRDO Profitability Grade

As expected, analysts are enthusiastic about the company’s earnings prospects, providing unanimously positive revisions. This vote of confidence, paired with CRDO’s stellar recent momentum, could spell potential for further upside as the company executes through 2026.

2. Micron Technology, Inc. (MU)

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductors

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 2 out of 537

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 1 out of 27

  • Market Capitalization: $311.03B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

I have written extensively about Micron, and its Quant story remains solid following its most recent earnings result, which incidentally provided a broad lift for the semiconductor industry. MU is another standout Alpha Pick and PQP stock that has returned 45% and 111% to the portfolios, respectively. Let’s start with the low-hanging fruit: Very few semiconductor stocks provide such an attractive entry point for investors. MU, a dominant player in the AI memory space, trades at an incredible 89% discount in terms of its FWD PEG. This discount is particularly remarkable in light of the stock’s superb price momentum, with shares returning 207% over the past year. Together, the delta between that valuation gap and performance strength suggests the market is underestimating its long-term earnings power.

MU Valuation Grade

The stock’s growth and profitability are also both ironclad. Highlights include a FWD EBITDA growth that's 581% above the tech sector, alongside a 23% ROE vs. the sector’s 6.30%. The company also sports a remarkable 53% EBITDA margin, significantly higher than tech stocks and its own five-year historical average.

MU Profitability Grade

MU’s recent wave of upward estimate revisions further complements its discounted valuation, growth, profitability, and momentum, making it a key leader in AI infrastructure as we head into 2026.

3. Willdan Group, Inc. (WLDN)

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Research and Consulting Services

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 4 out of 617

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 12/23/2025): 1 out of 39

  • Market Capitalization: $1.67B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

I like Willdan as an unconventional AI play. The company neither sells chips nor networking hardware, but instead tackles the energy efficiency challenge from a professional services and engineering angle. This positions the company to benefit from AI-driven power demand without competing directly in crowded semiconductor or cloud markets. It also makes the company more resilient to tech volatility: Willdan has weathered the late Q4 turbulence well, returning well above the market and broader tech sector (XLK) on a trailing one-month basis.

WLDN vs. S&P500 vs. XLK trailing one-month return

The company’s revenue lines are well diversified, with strategic acquisitions expanding its capabilities and geographic reach. This approach is reflected in the company’s ‘A’ Growth grade, which includes an FWD EPS growth that's 644% above the sector median.

WLDN Growth Grade

One benefit of Willdan’s public‑sector client base is that longer-duration contracts create a recurring revenue base. This helps protect the company from news-driven tech volatility and has shored up its profitability: WLDN sports an ROE that is 31% above the sector median. With solid fundamentals and stellar timeliness indicators, WLDN is a potentially overlooked AI play that could also serve as a diversifier in the New Year.

Concluding Summary

The Santa Claus rally has historically delivered positive returns. The rally, which failed to materialize in 2023 and 2024, has never missed for three consecutive years, typically favoring the dominant investment themes, which today are AI and cannabis stocks. SA Quant has identified four Strong Buy stocks for your Christmas wish list this year. Village Farms International, Credo Technology Group, Micron, and Willdan Group offer an outstanding blend of investment fundamentals, momentum, and earnings revisions grades across the cannabis and AI spaces.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using stock screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Consider using Seeking Alpha's 'Ratings Screener' tool to help find stocks that achieve diversification into the desired sectors you like. Alternatively, if you're looking for a select number of Quant Strong Buy recommendations on a monthly basis, you might want to explore Alpha Picks.

If you're looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It's a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps

data.title: First American: Improving Fundamentals At A Compelling Valuation
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Karandeep Kumar as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

While First American's (FAF) stock struggled this year (down ~2% YTD), the underlying business followed a different trajectory. Despite macroeconomic headwinds, the company delivered 41% revenue growth, or 14% adjusted revenue growth yoy in Q3, and improved profit margins in both the Title Insurance and Services segment as well as the Home Warranty segment.

Despite its resilience and growth trajectory, First American is trading at a market cap of $6.42 billion, a ~9.45% discount to its TTM revenue of $7.09 billion and a P/E of ~13.7x. A modest normalization in real estate activity, combined with continued capital returns, could drive meaningful upside for the company going forward.

Q3 results

First American delivered 42.3% revenue growth in its Title Insurance and Services segment and a 3.3% growth in its Home Warranty segment.

In Q3, refinance and commercial orders per day increased by 53.4% and 10.8%, respectively, whereas residential purchase orders declined by 3.7%, highlighting a weak residential transaction environment.

Revenues from Information and other services, including title search and related reports, title and other property records, non-insured settlement services, and risk mitigation products and services, increased 14.2% yoy in Q3.

The volume of orders per day, both open and closed, increased. It is worth noting that the average revenue per order (ARPO) declined 12.48% from $4,343 in Q4 2024 to $3,801 in Q3 2025. The management attributes this decline to "a shift in the mix from higher premium commercial transactions to lower premium refinance and default transactions." This shift offset the increasing commercial orders for the company.

However, the rising proportion of refinance activity also suggests that homeowners are increasingly responding to a more favourable rate environment, even as purchase activity remains constrained by affordability and inventory. This dynamic suggests improving borrower confidence and stabilizing housing conditions. An increase in residential real estate activity would help improve the ARPO for the company.

Improving Margins

For the Title Insurance and Services segment, which accounted for ~93% of the company's revenue, profit margins were 12.9% compared to -10.1% in Q3 last year. It is important to note that in Q3 last year, the company posted a $311.5 million investment loss attributable to "the Company's investment portfolio rebalancing project," of which $308 million was reflected in this segment. Adjusted for such loss, the margins would have been ~10% last year, highlighting operational strength and continued improvements in the MRQ.

Margins in the Home Warranty segment improved by 6.0% to 14.1% in the MRQ from 8.1% in Q3 last year, mostly due to a ~10% reduction in loss provisions attributable to lower claims frequency. A large portion of revenues in this segment comes from renewals rather than real estate activity in the year.

Almost all valuation and profitability ratios indicated a positive trend in the underlying business.

Dividend & Share Buybacks

First American pays a $0.55 quarterly dividend (~3.44% yield), which has grown 19.5% over the last five years and 2% in the past year. With a payout ratio of 46.67%, the dividend is considerably safe for income-oriented investors. The company has been consistently buying back its stock, reducing the number of shares outstanding to 101.9 million in Q3 2025 from 103.0 million in Q3 2024 (a decrease of ~1.07%), with a similar trend expected in the future.

Key Risks

First American and its peers are sensitive to interest rates, real estate activity, and the regulatory environment. While the consensus outlook expects momentum in commercial, industrial and residential transactions, a prolonged stagnation in demand can put downward pressure on the company. As previously discussed, the compression of Average Revenue Per Order (ARPO) is a key concern for investors in First American, which may persist if the new transaction activity doesn't grow as expected.

However, it should also be noted that while the ARPO declined, the volume of total open orders and closed orders per day has been growing steadily (up ~15% and ~16% respectively), which propelled the revenue growth for First American. On the other hand, the commercial ARPO hit a record $16,000 in Q3, driven by strong industrial demand.

Regulatory Risks

Heading into 2026, First American is navigating an increasingly dense regulatory landscape, characterized by heightened federal oversight and new reporting mandates. A primary headwind is the FinCEN Residential Real Estate Rule, which requires title companies to act as the lead "reporting person" for non-financed (all-cash) residential transfers to legal entities or trusts. While some reporting deadlines were recently pushed to March 1, 2026, the operational burden of identifying beneficial owners behind shell companies adds immediate administrative friction. Furthermore, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) continues to scrutinize "junk fees" within mortgage closing costs. This scrutiny could lead to mandatory fee-transparency changes or even a shift toward lender-paid title insurance models, potentially disrupting traditional pricing power. In Canada, the FINTRAC reporting obligations for title insurers further underscore a global trend toward using title insurers as frontline anti-money laundering (AML) gatekeepers.

Such regulatory requirements are expected to drag on operational margins. Large-scale data reporting requires ongoing investments in software (as reflected in First American's investment and capitalization of its software costs in Q3 2025) and legal staff, which may slightly weigh on Title segment margins in the near term. Conversely, these complex regulations favour "The Big Four," such as First American. Smaller, regional title agencies often lack the technological infrastructure (such as FAF's Endpoint or Sequoia) to automate these federal reporting requirements, which likely drives further market consolidation toward FAF.

Valuation

To determine a fair value for First American, I will use the consensus EPS data from Seeking Alpha.

While the market is in the earlier stage of the real estate recovery cycle, the stock trades at a P/E of ~13.7x. However, as the market eventually recovers and stabilizes, I expect the company to trade at a P/E of at least 15x.

Using the consensus EPS of $6.32 for 2027 as a basis for our calculation, we will apply a discount rate of 9% and a P/E ratio of 15 to determine the present value of the stock, which comes to $79.80 (~24% discount to price at closing), excluding the dividend. However, if we include the cash distributions (no reinvestments), the fair value comes to $83.75 (79.80+3.95)(~27% discount to price at closing)

Note: For dividends, we use the TTM dividend of $2.18 and estimate a 2% annual growth rate.

Future Value PV (r=9%)
Year 1 $2.22 $2.04
Year 2 $2.27 $1.91
Total $4.49 $3.95

Outlook

On the operational side, continued improvement in margins and revenue growth is expected as real estate activity regains momentum, interest rates decline, and markets stabilize. Continued investments in data centers and "Re-industrialization of America" are expected to contribute meaningfully to the ARPO in the future.

The information and other revenues segment, which includes data services and is often overlooked, is expected to create greater value for the company. Although these revenues currently represent a small percentage of overall revenues, they are less volatile due to a significant portion coming from subscription fees.

Given its current valuation, improving revenues, and favourable macroeconomic tailwinds, I assign a BUY rating to the company.

data.title: Alibaba: Consumer Slowdown Weighs On Cloud Intelligence
data.description:
Chinese consumption and global e-commerce are cooling off

Alibaba Group's (BABA) cloud service is growing strongly. That's how I interpreted it when I looked at Q2 2026 (Sep 2025), where the segment's growth was 34% year-over-year. I saw it as something very important because retail consumption in China, along with GDP growth, is slowing down, and the projections are not good.

E-commerce is still something key for the company because it represents 54% of the revenue, if we only take China. I saw something else: seller services account for 60% of Chinese commerce and are almost double the revenue of the cloud segment. Furthermore, if I also include international e-commerce, the broadline segment represents approximately 68% of total revenue. Cloud revenues represent only 16%, but in Q2 2026 it proved to be the fastest-growing segment by a wide margin (Slide 15 of the quarterly report).

If we only take a look at 54% of that revenue, you can see it totally depends on China. And the projections aren't that good. The outlook for Chinese consumption became bleak (November saw the lowest level of consumption in three years), so the government launched a new five-year plan to revitalize consumption in the coming years. But that didn't change the growth outlook.

I know economists pay close attention to the rate of consumption when calculating future GDP. And that's okay. Because Chinese consumption, for example, represents 56% of the GDP. But I'm analyzing it in reverse. With a slowing GDP, as is expected in China for the next few years, it is difficult for consumption to grow much.

I am part of the group that believes consumption is a key part of countries' GDP. Savings, productivity, and especially the purchasing power of household income are what determine future consumption. In China, based on these data, I can't see very ambitious projections.

And besides, I found something very striking. E-commerce is not growing at the same speed as in years before. During the Covid pandemic there was a boom in e-commerce, and we still maintain this behavior of buying online. But in net terms, it seems that enthusiasm is slowing down. I believe there are a few reasons. The return to physical stores and the difficulty of finding new users are, I think, two major factors.

The weak Chinese consumption and the global deceleration of e-commerce won't favor Alibaba. Therefore, I see the cloud segment as a key part of my long-term investment plans, and since I haven't yet found strong and clear catalysts in that regard, I prefer to rate the company as a Hold.

Investments and margin reductions in Q2 2026

Alibaba is making a very big investment in AI to adapt its service to the growing demand. The AI investment is focused on improving e-commerce, but the focus is mostly on the cloud service. Over the last four quarters, Alibaba spent RMB 120 billion on AI infrastructure, while revenue in the same period was RMB 1.012 billion. The projected future CAPEX is RMB 380 billion, although the company does not rule out increasing investment if demand warrants it.

I also think these expenses, although necessary due to the high competition in the cloud sector globally, are reducing margins in the short term. This isn't something that worries me because I think it's a need and something hyperescalers are doing too. Even so, I believe it's worth it to follow its development very closely. In other words, how will it impact the strong investment on the margins and the free cash flow trend?

In the quarter, the EBIT margin was 2%, the lowest number since March 2021 and far below the previous quarter. This was due, as I understand it, to the RMB 50 billion subsidy plan announced last July. I'm assuming that part of that amount was added to the sales expenses, which grew strongly year-on-year, from RMB 32 billion to RMB 66 billion. This increase reduced margins. I must admit I don't like these subsidy plans; therefore, 2026 will be a year in which I want to see a solid return to profitability. By the way, its net income also fell hard, 53%.

Meanwhile, the cash position also deteriorated, with free cash flow being negative in the quarter by RMB 21 billion, RMB 3 billion more than in the previous quarter. In this case, it is due to the CAPEX growth. In the quarter, the CAPEX was RMB 31 billion, double that of the same period in the previous year.

Cloud profitability: A path to consolidation or still slow growth?

But leaving aside the fall in profitability and the investment efforts, let's now analyze the possible consolidation of the cloud segment of Alibaba. A question I asked myself was: could the company maintain its 35% YoY growth in the segment? Or was it something casual? When I looked at the performance of the last quarter, the first impression I had was that it could have been a very casual jump.

However, if you take a look at the previous chart, you'll see that, since the last quarter of 2024, the segment has reached double-digit YoY growth. I think the combination I made with the e-commerce segment also helped, since we can make a leap in growth compared to other segments. So I was asking myself if this cloud growth is the beginning of the consolidation or just a casual jump.

To better understand it, I went to check the YoY growth in the last presented quarter of that segment for the big world players: Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Alphabet (GOOG). In Q3 2025, the YoY growth they had was 20%, 39%, and 34%, respectively. AMZN is a very special case because it dominates the cloud service with its AWS segment, with a leading market share of 29%, and it already represents more than half of its revenue, compared to e-commerce.

Alibaba is not in a bad position in the cloud, but it is still very far from the American giants, in my opinion. Its market share is 4%, but it has been decreasing in the last few years. Something positive I thought was that Alibaba could still have plenty of room to grow to levels of 34% or more in Cloud Intelligence. Large investments and negative free cash flow could be working in the medium term, and that would indicate that they will continue to work in the future.

Alibaba directors were consulted about this in the conference call. In fact, a Morgan Stanley assistant asked a very interesting question connected to my own doubts.

Morgan Stanley: How should we look at the growth outlook going forward? Should we continue to expand growth to accelerate?

Yongming Wu, CEO: Certainly, we see that customer demand for AI is -- remains very strong. In fact, we're not even able to keep pace with the growth in customer demand as in orders in terms of the pace at which we can deploy new servers. So we certainly do see the demand for AI is accelerating.

In terms of where that demand is coming from, it's really coming from all aspects of enterprise operations as AI adoption continues to not only accelerate but deepen with applications across product development throughout manufacturing processes and also in terms of supporting the enterprises and customers use their products.

I often hear that some cloud companies can't keep up with orders and have to invest more money in AI infrastructure. So I think it's true: there is a strong catalyst for Alibaba. However, let's suppose the company could keep this 34% growth rhythm: there's still a long way to go from being able to surpass more than half of e-commerce revenue. This transition could be accelerated if e-commerce income decreases, but this won't be something good for the business. It will depend, then, on maintaining a high rate of growth in the cloud.

At a rate of 34% annually, compared to 5-8% for e-commerce, Alibaba's cloud segment could take 5-6 years to become the most important source of total revenue. We must add something very important: AI Chinese infrastructure is not the same as the American. For example, here comes another question: could Alibaba maintain that rhythm in a context of supply shortages of advanced AI chips? That's something Yongming Wu talked about during the conference call.

And with all of this rapid growth in demand, we also need to be thinking about the supply side. I'm sure that you, as analysts have also been looking at the supply side. Starting from the second half of this year, I think we've seen worldwide. If you look at fabs, if you look at DRAM vendors, storage companies, CPU manufacturers across all of those different links in the value chain that go to making AI servers, there is a situation of undersupply. Supply is unable to keep up with demand for all of these components globally.

And I think that you can expect that to continue throughout this scaling up an investment cycle, driven by real demand for AI, we know that the supply side is going to be a relatively large bottleneck. So I think that it could be at least a period of 2 or 3 years for those different suppliers, those different venues to be able to ramp up their production capacity.

...and what we can see internally in the industry, and if we look at the hyperscalers in the U.S., all of the latest GPUs that are running are running at full capacity and not just them, the last generation GPUs, even GPUs from 3 to 5 years ago, so also several generations back, those GPUs are to this day still running at full capacity.

In summary, today Alibaba's cloud segment is something that excites me, but I still need to see more strength in its weight in its share of income to invest in the long term.

The multiples are above their historical average, and I see the 2020 price as very far off

The company will close this year with a 78% increase in its share price, surpassing cloud leaders like Amazon, Microsoft, and Google. My feeling is that this year the market rewarded the expectations of the cloud potential, and the price went again to the 2022 highs, but I still see the 2020 price of approximately $300 as a long way off. Despite the great performance, the margin reduction and the fall of the free cash flow were something that caused some doubts, in my opinion.

When I looked at the valuation ratios, I found P/E non-GAAP is not only higher than the median sector, but it's also very well above the 5-year average: 22.7x vs. 13.8x. I saw something similar in EV/EBITDA FWD. It's 15.8x vs. the 5-year average of 10.3x, a difference of 53.8%.

For its part, revenue growth projections are well below the 5-year average: 7.8x vs. 14.7%. I saw the same with expected earnings per share, at 2.7%, below the sector average of 5.4% and the 5-year average of 7.4%. These projections are explained by the weak Chinese market consumption and the slowdown in e-commerce, while the potential of the cloud, which I believe was priced in by 2025, isn't yet reflected in the revenue projections. With these estimates and these valuation multiples, I believe Hold is the best option.

Weak consumer spending and the risks of relying on US chips

I outlined the risks for Alibaba throughout the article, but I think it's worth taking a close look at both China's economic performance in the coming years and the company's ability to improve and expand its cloud service. In part, that capacity will be dependent on the AI infrastructure. In that context, the market relationships between China and the US will be very important.

Alibaba, like other Chinese companies such as Tencent (TCEHY) and Baidu (BIDU), uses Nvidia (NVDA) chips and had been accumulating them before the export bans that Donald Trump imposed. As I understand it, they mix those chips with local semiconductors. With the recent lifting of the ban on the sale of Nvidia's H200, an opportunity arises for Alibaba. However, this is still a risk. In other words, China's inability to manufacture more advanced chips limits the AI ​​potential of its companies.

Another risk I see for Alibaba is connected to the weakness of domestic consumption that, if it continues, could devolve into new subsidy programs. As I've shown you, the subsidy and the CAPEX increase are already pressing the margins. CAPEX is a catalyst; that goes without saying. But combined with higher expenses and less efficiency, it could complicate the profitability in the short term, and therefore, it could reduce the shareholders profits.

Summary

Alibaba is a leader in e-commerce in China, which is no small feat for an economic and population powerhouse like the Asian giant. However, both consumer growth and e-commerce growth are slowing down. I see the drop in consumption as a logical slowdown given China's GDP projections for the coming years.

At the same time, global e-commerce is no longer growing at the levels seen during the pandemic. As e-commerce represents almost 68% of Alibaba's revenue, the reconversion towards the Cloud Intelligence segment is an operational need. But it still has a long way to go. I believe the growth in the share price in 2025 was due to those expectations. Under these premises, with a potential partly discounted in the price, and with my doubts about the cloud potential, my rating for Alibaba is Hold.

data.title: British American Tobacco: The Case For Its Smokeless Future
data.description:
Introduction

British American Tobacco (BTI) is the last remaining giant of the tobacco industry that I have to review. With its current strategy of "A Better Tomorrow" and the current dynamics of the tobacco industry, I want to see how BTI fares against its rivals, Philip Morris and Altria, and see if it is well-positioned to capture the future of the tobacco industry.

BTI currently has a wide portfolio of brands in both new categories, such as nicotine pouches with VELO, the up-and-coming rival of ZYN, as well as traditional cigarettes with well-known brands such as Kent and Lucky Strike.

Current Dynamics & Segment Overview

BTI, being based in the UK, does not release quarterly earnings; therefore, I’ll base what has been going on in the business on the recent outlook update. BTI is now expecting a 2% growth in revenue and overall operating profit, which has been the relative trend for the tobacco industry in general, with many looking at the growth segment to offset the structural decline in cigarette brands. Going deeper, current CEO Tadeu Marroco stated that Vuse and the nicotine pouch segment have seen volume and revenue improvements in the US, which is encouraging, though the Vapour category continues to be impacted by illicit proliferation. All of this has pushed BTI to expand its share buyback program to £1.3B for FY2026. Moreover, the company has maintained its operating cash flow conversion rate of over 95%, which highlights how the company has championed highly disciplined capital management.

Now I want to go segment by segment so that investors can grasp the current state of BTI’s operation. The current star of the portfolio in my mind is akin to ZYN for PM, VELO, which has driven the surge in the company's stock price. Currently, the VELO Plus line has been gaining high traction in the US, seeing triple-digit revenue growth in the second half of 2025, and it has been gaining market share at a rapid pace in the nicotine pouch industry. This line is using synthetic nicotine instead of nicotine derived from tobacco plants; thus, it offers a cleaner chemical profile than other products on shelves. The experience is apparently also better with its wet pouch design and instant release of nicotine effects, which should gain traction among users (I’m certainly not pushing the use of nicotine products, but I’m just stating how BTI could gain an advantage over competitors). The overall segment has reached full-year profitability in 2025, which is far ahead of what many analysts were expecting.

On the e-cigarette front, or the Vapour segment, it includes the Vuse product lines, which have seen a roller coaster in terms of revenue for the year as the industry is fighting against illegal disposable vapes that have been a drag against legal and FDA-approved brands like Vuse. The FDA has begun to act majorly against these, which is why the segment has recovered with its dominant market share in the US. In a similar segment, there are also heated products with brands like glo and neo that have seen relatively flat performance as BTI is facing major competition from PM’s IQOS, mainly in Japan. To counteract this, BTI is focusing on the rollout of glo Hilo, which would be a premium offering for BTI. I do not expect this segment to be a major driver of earnings for the company, though I understand that it needs to have a presence in the market to fight off PM.

And now on to the legacy core with traditional cigarettes that include many in-house brands, as previously mentioned, as well as the rights to Camel and Newport in the US. The industry has been grappling with lower volumes that are set to decline by around 2% annually for the whole industry, yet BTI and its peers have been successfully using their pricing power, thanks to the premium perception of their brands, to maintain actual revenue growth. Looking at the US, BTI is actually seeing a rebound, with the segment returning to revenue and profit growth for the year, thanks to its constant market share. Shining brands in the portfolio include Newport, with its dominant position in the menthol category despite having ongoing regulatory threats. Others would be Lucky Strike and Natural American Spirit, which have also been stars in 2025 as they have been gaining market share thanks to their appeal to different consumers who are more value-seekers.

BTI has gone through a major accounting shift to amortize the value of the company’s US cigarette brands, giving most of them a 30-year life span, acknowledging that these will eventually have zero value in the future, and aligning the company with its motto of being smokeless by 2035. Mainly, this segment acts as the big cash cow thanks to its immense FCF generation that is able to fund the expansion into smokeless products as well as strengthen the balance sheet and return excesses to shareholders.

Valuation

At first, BTI looks relatively expensive with its P/E standing at nearly 30, but it is important to note that this is mainly due to impairment charges. Right now, BTI trades at a forward P/E of 12.54, which is much more in line with the industry. Regarding FCF, it has also turned negative due to a “once-in-a-generation” legal event that was the Canadian Tobacco Litigation Settlement. The Ontario Superior Court approved a C$32.5B settlement in which the companies were required to pay out nearly all of their cash on hand upfront. Furthermore, there will be an 85% profit sweep going directly to settlement payments for years to come in Canada. By taking an adjusted look at FCF, overlooking the settlement, BTI is expected to generate £7.04B for 2025 and has been trading at around a 15% FCF yield over the past 7 years. Another argument would be its generous dividend yield, currently standing at 5.4%, which allows investors to be patient with BTI.

Growth for the company is now inadvertently tied to the smokeless transformation, which is a good thing. The company is now entering a quality growth phase where it expects significant acceleration of its financial performance starting in 2026. Both the regulatory cleanup for Vuse and the launch of VELO Plus should be a great tailwind, as well as cigarettes being able to offset declining volumes. This has been factored into the stock already, with its peer-leading 55.45% YTD return, the largest among its peers, but I still see some upside as BTI realizes the upside of its strategy. Looking ahead, I want to see how VELO fares against ZYN and if it can continue to take market share away from the dominant competitor in the space.

BTI currently has some of the best margins in the industry, potentially because it was further behind in terms of smokeless products compared to Philip Morris, which tends to have lower margins than cigarettes. With the smokeless product range taking on a larger share of revenue, the company should see its overall profitability dip, although I’d rather have a sustainable business with lower margins than a dying one with high margins. The transition phase means that BTI can use the high level of cash flows from cigarettes to finance its growth products. Mainly, Nicotine pouches will certainly have the best margin prospects in my opinion, as they are simple dry goods that don’t require Batteries or complex electronics like vapour & heated tobacco. Furthermore, with the company’s plan to save around £2B by 2030 through supply chain automation and office streamlining, we could still see incremental margin growth.

Risks

The most imminent risk to investing in BTI would be the structural decline of cigarettes, which are still a core revenue driver. Right now, BTI can offset the volume decline with price hikes, but soon there will be a tipping point when consumers will accelerate the trend to go towards cheaper alternatives, stop smoking, or, most favorably for BTI, go towards smokeless options.

Litigation is the other main issue, as highlighted by the most recent Canadian lawsuits. The operations in the country will already be almost non-profitable, with 85% of Canadian net income swept away. It is a specific case, but it sets a precedent for many countries that could see massive class-action settlements.

Conclusion

BTI’s future is now heavily tilted towards smokeless alternatives, which is a relatively good thing. Margins will be slightly compressed, but the company has the dominant position in e-cigarettes and is rapidly catching up to Zyn in the nicotine pouches area. With its high dividend yield and the narrative already switching to the potential of its smokeless products, I believe that British American Tobacco is a Buy.
